CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We haven’t read much about the English Railway
trouble, but we see that a Mr. Bell, ‘“President and
Organiser ”’ of a British Trades Union, is over in this
country on the stump. We hope the railway workers
in Ireland won’t be led away by this Mr. Bell from
Britain into striking. Heaven knows our railways are
slow enough without attempting to stop them altogether
for a time. \Why, as we have long ago asked, do not
the railway workers in Ireland organise an independent
Irish Trades Union of their own? We are ?,gainst the
absorption of Irish railways by English’ Railway Com-
panies—an absorption that has already gone too far;
and we are also against Irish ticket checkers, porters,
etc., being swallowed up by-a British Trades Unm!:.
If the Englishmen want to strike, let them—that’s
their business; but industry and trade are b_s.d enough
in Ireland without attempting to dislocate it further.
What do the Irish railway workers want? Probably,
like everyone else, not excluding editors, they wgnt more
money if they can get it—but can they get it? We
don’t know exactly what are the grievances of the Irish
railway workers at the moment—perhaps they don’t
exactly know themselves, and it may be that Mr. Be_ll
from England is over here instructing them in their
grievances.

We see that Mr. Bell from England was in Waterford
recently, and he was met at the station by a crowd
which included a brass and reed band, and there was a
torchlight procession as if it had been a new Thomas
Francis Meagher rather than an English Trades Union
Organiser who had visited the City. It appears that
Mr. Bell from England spoke at length ‘“ on the railway
men’s national all-grade movement.’’ We wonder
what does the ‘‘ national ’’ stand for? According to a
report of his speech, he said :—*‘ The first step towards
guaranteeing the national all-grade movement was to
get together the largest and most important conference
of railway men yet held in the history of railways.
That conference was held at Birmingham in November
last, and 600 delegates assembled to draw up a pro-
gramme suitable for England and Wales.”” So the
conference was held in Birmingham, where so many
dumps come from that draw protests from Irish work-
ingmen. It is bad enough to have locomotives and
railway carriages coming into Ireland from abroad with-
out having even our railway strikes hatched in Birm-
ingham.

The poor old ‘ Freeman ’’ of the dwindling dividends
is to be pitied in its efforts to bury its head in the sands
and ‘“let on’’ that there is no such thing as the “little”
Lraper! Last week our readers saw that as soon as the
paper wkich the Editor of this journal read at the
Munster Feis came on the ‘“ Daily Dwindler ”’ ceased re-
porting the Munster Feis. Our distinguished journal-
istic friend, Mr. J. M. Wall, of New York, will no
doubt be shocked at the ‘ Dwindler’s’’ attempt at
dodging the LEADER ; but it only amuses us, and what is
more it advertises us! Recently the vile name of the
LEADER appeared a few times in the ‘ Daily Dwindler.”
The ‘‘superior ”’ Mr. Stephen Gwynn dragged it into
his recent ‘‘ O’Growney for two and coffee for one’’
article, and the poor ‘“ Freeman >’ was evidently afraid
to tamper with the copy of the member for Galway, who
trades under the name of Smith O’Brien. Mr. John
Sweetman, who never recommended anyone to go to jail
though he is a vice-president of the so-called ‘‘ National
Council,”” dragged in the LEADER into a letter of his,
and strange to relate the vile name passed the ‘‘ Dwind-
ler’s’” censor. But there was probably a row in the
office, and no doubt the sub-editor immediately respon-
sible was instructed, ‘‘ let it not occur again.””  Well,
though Mr. John Sweetman has no intention of pas-
sively resisting the Income Tax collector, as his furni-
ture would be seized, he still keeps on writing letters.
Talk is cheap whatever .may be said about
having your furniture seized or going to jail.  Mr.
Sweetman sent off a letter, dated September 24th, to
the ‘ Freeman,”” and apparently he also sent a copy
to the ‘‘ Independent.”” The vile name of the LEADER
appeared in this epistle from Meath where the grass
grows to the ““ truly nationals.”” We can picture the
scene of consternation in the office of the “ Dwindler.””
In our mind’s eye we see Mr. Brayden tearing his beard
out by the handful, his eyes rolling in a fine frenzy of
anything but poetry. We almost see in our mind’s eye
the old drab offices in Prince’s Street shake as the cap-
tain of the ‘“ Dwindler *’ shouts: ‘‘ All hands on deck—
come up here, Matty! ”’

We are not particularly interested in Mr. Sweetman’s
views on affairs,’ as we don’t think he takes himself
seriously, and we are quite sure that we don’t—an
apostle of a passive resistance policy who pays up his
income tax, as if he didn’t his pots and kettles and

.skillets might be seized, is a subject for comedy—and

broad comedy at that. But, anyway, in' the version of
Mr. Sweetman’s letter that appeared in the ‘ Indepen-
dent ”” - the following sentence occurs:—‘‘ Evidently,
neither Moses nor the editor of the LEADER were in it

with John Redmond, but all this seems to be a positive
proof of the truth of the Sinn Fein view, that Ireland
can get nothing from England through the efforts of a
Parliamentary Party.”” We don’t exactly know what
Mr. Sweebman’s point is, nor do we see the connection
between Moses and our unworthy selves—but Mr.
Sweetman’s point, whatever it may be, has nothing to
do with our point about the ¢ Dwindler.”” When we
turn to the letter, as it appears in the ‘‘ Freeman ’’ qf
the dwindling dividends, we find the sentence reads this
way :—“ All this seems to be a positive proof of the
truth of the Sinn Fein view, that Ireland can geb
nothing from England through the efforts of a Parlia-
mentary Party.”’

It will be seen that the ‘ Dwindler’’ deliberately
smashed up a sentence in order to exclude that night-
mare to it whick: is called the ‘ LEaper.”” It doesn’t
mind a reference to the Martynettes—they are polished
off, and may be considered as back numbers, and it
dces not matter mentioning them, but the very name
of the very much alive LeapErR makes Prince’s Street
squirm. But what of Moses? Surely there must have
been a council of war—we have shunted off the nautical
metaphor—as to the means by which the ‘‘ LrapEr
could be cut out witkout doing violence to Moses. We
take it that the combined wisdom of the staff on the
floor of the dwindling dividends was not equal to the
task, and so Moses was sent into the wilderness with
the editor of the Leaper! Oh, what a press! Is it
any wonder that the Irish Party and the orthodox
Nationalist politicians are a falling market like the
‘“ Freeman *’ shares wken this sort of policy of dodging
the drops in a shower of rain is pursued by the ‘‘ Free-
man’s Journal.”’ Tt has its sad and serious as well as
its ridiculous side. Here is the LeapER that has made
bistory, and that has enthusiastic supporters in every
part of Ireland, and in England, Scotland, Wales, Aus-
tralia, America, and other countries, and this *“ leading
daily *’ paper, when it is put to it, turns out even Moses
rather than print the terrible phrase—‘‘ TLe Editor of
the ‘ LEADER!” "’

In the course of a letter dated September 17, 1907,
Mr. David J. Costello, 2777 Pine Street, San Francisco,
writes:—‘“T am much disappointed that we have not
a daily LEADER for Ireland, as it is the firm conviction of
many practical and thinking men in this country that
yecur weekly LEADER has done more for Ireland since its
appearance than has all the other so-called national
papers in the country. It has advocated temperance,
Lome manufacture and self-reliance generally, which
will help more than any will-o’-the-wisp theories of
politicians to make the country prosperous. By tem-
perance you will keep millions at home instead of put-
ting it into the English Exchequer for their soldiers to
fight poor savages in various parts of the globe who
struggle for their country’s rights, and home manufac-
turing will keep many a poor family at home in com-
fort who have to come to tkis and other countries for a
living.””

The Sligo Technical Instruction Committee recently
advertised for an Art Master. ~Amongst ten who
applied for the post, only one applicant was Irish, and
he, we understand, was disqualified through inability
to teach woodcarving. At the suggestion of Dr.
Clancy, Bishop of Elphin, the Committee decided not
to fill the vacancy without making a.npthef effort to
obtain- an Irishman, and so the position is now re-
advertised. The salary is £100 per annum, and the
successful applicant must be fully qualified in the usual
Art subjects. A qualification to teach woodcarving
will be an advantage. Surely there ought to be some-
one in Ireland able and willing to fill this job.

A reader has sent us a paper called ‘“The Vanguard,”
which in turn was sent to him by some person or per-
sons unknown. It is a ‘‘religious’’ paper, the Chris-
tian tone of which may be gathered from such
a phrase in the editor’s notes as ‘‘the plague of
Popery.”’  We have something else to do beside looking
through that sort of stuff, but we were interested to see
an advertisement in the paper about Lord Ashtown’s
notorious ‘‘ Grievances from Ireland.”” Subscriptions
in aid of that delectable publication may be sent either
to the ‘‘TImperial Protestant Federation,”” or to the
Right Hon. Lord Ashtown, Woodlawn, Co. Galway.
There is a list of the Patrons of ‘‘ Grievances’’ also
given. They include the Marquis of Sligo, Earl Mount-
cashell, Lady Templemore, Lady Anne Trench, Sir
Frederick William Shaw, Bart., J.P., Sir James Hen-
derson, J.P., D.L., Captain Robert Wade Thompson,
J.P., Professor Bunnell Lewis, M.A., F.S.A., etc. The
last named was formerly of Queen’s College, Cork.

Last week we asked who was Judge Fitzgerald? We
hayve received some interesting particulars since about
‘“ Ragamuffin Fitzy,” as he might not inappropriately
be called. His father, the late Judge J. D. Fitzgerald,
was once member for Ennis. As he got up the ladder,
instead of a warm shake hands, he gave certain of his
acquaintances his one finger, and, in consequence of
that condescending habit, he got the name, One-finger
Jack.””  ‘“Ragamuffin Fitzy ” was sent to England to
school—a college in Ireland was not good euoaugh, we
suppose. FHe was sent to Oscott, which was then con-
sidered one of the.most ‘“ tony ’’ Catholic Colleges in
England., We suppose ‘“One-finger Jack’’ was one
of the fellows that O’Connell emancipated !

‘“ Dundanian ”’ sends us the following appreciation of
Canon O’Leary at the Munster Feis:— But to me
the chief interest lay in the presence and the oration of
the now celebrated author of ¢ Seadna,” the Very Rev.
Canon O’Leary, more familiarly known as ¢ An T-athair
Peadar.” I had not yet seen him in the flesh, although
I had read with delight many of his splendid books.
One likes to know what the appearance of one’s
favourite author is like, and in the absence of the man
himself, one has too often to be satisfied with a photo-
graph. Father O’Leary’s photograph I had frequently
seen, and, judging therefrom, I expected to see a tall,
stout, broad-shouldered clergyman. But the author
of ‘Seadna’ is neither tall nor great-shouldered. On
the contrary, he is rather low-sized, and is not in any
way overburthened with flesh. He wears glasses, which
in no way dim the light in the sharp, keen eyes. His
hair is quite white, but sparse in quantity, and as he
stood speaking on the platform, his broad, white gleam-
ing forehead formed a very fine feature of his appear-
ance. His face bears the healthy signs of a life spent
i1 the country, and, moreover, it tells of great strength,
of keen intelligence, and of a great fund of humour.
Even in repose it is full of character, but when it passes
rapidly through the thousand phases of expression, re-
sponsive to the various thoughts contained in a brilliant,
vigorous speech, it is then well worthy of study indeed.
And what a speech! One has to hear such a consum-
mate master of the language in order to understand
fully the  Ceol "—the music and the power of the Irish
tongue. Accompanying his remarks with an abundance
of telling gestures, the great writer pours forth his
words in an eloquent stream, securing for each phrase
or sentence its full effect. At one moment the utter-
ance is slow and deliberate—each point is urged in
measured tones, and a significant clap of one hand on
the other seems to drive the argument well home; but,
the next instant warmed into mighty energy by some
flash of emotion, the voice passes with wondrous
swiftness through the whole range of its tones up to the
high-pitched and vigorous enforcement of some par-
ticularly important fact. And between these two limits
—between the easy, quict language of reasoning and
the rapid, swift-running current when deeply-felt
thoughts come to be expressed—there are a whole host
of moods in which Canon O’Leary demonstrates his
own genius, and demonstrates, too, the flexibility of

the tongue of the Gael, and its music and its power.
The principal matter of the speech was the manner 1n
which ¢ An T-athair Peadar’ dwelt on the value of the
young people in the movement. Now, he asserted, was
the time to bring them in, when they could acquire
the language with comparative ease, and then in after
years they could be depended on to do their part in
the work. What valuable services they would then be
able to render, especially the ‘ Cailini’ whom he saw in
such numbers before him, and their sisters also through-
out the land. The ‘sean-daoine’ (the old people)
would, in the course of nature, be soon disappearing
from the stage of life, and it would be for the Cailini
and the Buachailli—the boys and the girls—to carry
the Celtic flag forward to success. As he uttered the
word ‘Sean-daoine’ (old people), the Canon gave an
cloquent sweep of his hand backward to indicate the
gentlemen gathered with him on the platform. There
was a gleam of humour in his eye as he did so, for he
was aware that many of those behind him would still
be very slow to admit the presence of any tokens of
age on their well-preserved persons. It was a daring
stroke, but it was highly successful, and the best part of
ils success lay in the surprise and curiosity with which
those who did not understand Irish observed the
amusement among those who did. Then it was :cx-
plained to them how unblushingly to their faces they
had been called *‘pean-oaoine,” and a quiet laugh
passed round among them at Canon O’Leary’s audacious
little joke.”

At the recent prize day in St. Angela’s College and
High School, Cork, His Lordship, Most Rev. Dr. O’Cal-
laghan, was present and distributed the prizes to the
successful students. We are glad to note that the
College has had a very successful educational year. At
the Royal University Examinations, one student took
a degree on the Honour Course in Modern Language
and Literature, and the three students presented for
Second Arts were successful. Three out of four stu-
dents passed in First Arts, and six matriculated. In
the Junior Grade Intermediate, a pupil of St. Angelas
obtained third place in all Ireland for Irish, being only
one mark behind second place. A pupil in the Middle
Grade won a £3 prize for Irish Composition. Among
the distinctions in the Senior Grade are lst prize for
excellence in the Grade; £5 prize for German Lan-
guage and Literature ; 3rd place in Ireland for Drawing,
etc. The Bronze Medal for Experimental Science is
won from all Ireland in the Middle Grade with the
additional distinction of first place.  Details of the
College’s successes will be found in the Annual Report
which we publish in another column.

There is to be, or has been, a concert at the Loreto
Convent, Youghal. Youghal is in quite an Irish-speak-
ing part of the country, or, anyway, in a part where a
lot of people could speak Irish if they wished to do so.
The programme is before us, and certainly it is not one
that would tend to encourage people who can talk Irish
to talk it. We often feel it very trying on the nerves
to keep in a state of being moderate. We preached
some moderation recently, and the next day we were
so circumstanced that we felt the moderation very hard
to live up to. There is not one song in Irish in this
programme of the Loreto Convent, Youghal. There
are several songs witk' foreign names, but we are not so
learned in modern languages as the pupils of the
Youghal Loreto Convent, so we could not place the
names with perfect confidence in their various
nationalities. If you want to know all about Europe,
go to Youghal—or perhaps at the Convent they call it
You-gall, like the importations. This Youghal Loreto
Convent programme is farcical. There are Franciscan
Monks in Mount Bellew teaching students how to farm,
and Nuns at Dunmanway teaching girls how to sew
and feed pigs and useful things of that sort, but at
You-gall Loreto Convent School the Irish language is
boycotted in the programme that is decorated with such
words as ‘‘ Masaneillo,”” ‘‘La Paloma,” ‘“In der
Miihle.”” There is a piano and violin item
of ‘“Sing me to sleep.”” The Loreto Convent at You-
gall ought to wake up or else we may mnot be able to
keep being moderate. >

was much struck by ‘Lee’s’ very .
able critique on Music and Paiut’mg at the Munster
Teis,”” which appears in last week’s ]EA‘EADER,'bl.lt was
considerably astonished at his query— How is :1’1; that ]
we fail to produce native organists in Treland ? |

The fact is, we do not fail to produce them,‘ we 1
“only” fail to employ them. The talent ‘andvabxhty
are here in abundance, the means of cultivation are
here also, but when a vacancy doeg occur, _wlnch_ would
give an Irish organist an opportunity of displaying the
aforesaid talent and ability, he must respectfully stand
aside to make room for outsiders, and, as a resu.l(: of .
this ““ No Irish need apply (at least at home) policy,” 1
nearly all the great churches in America have Irishmen |
as organists.

This is more curious from the fact that, there, they
are in open competition with a great mumber of con-
tinental organists, and even the fact that the majority
of them are (like myself) afflicted with aggressively
Trish names, does not seem in the least to affect their
prestige.

I may also add that I have in my possession a letter
received last November from Corrington, U.S.A., offer-
ing me the position there as Cathedral Organist, and
were it not for the fact that I was one of the singularly
fortunate ‘‘ Irish *’ organists who have got a position in
““Ireland,” I may have hesitated to refuse such an i
offer.—Yours faithfully, il
EUGENE S. O'MAHONY,

IRELAND AT WORK.

KILKENNY FURNITURE.

E have passed by Kilkenny scores of times in

the train, but it was not until one day re-

cently that we trod its streets. Until a year or so ago,
Kilkenny was famous, and justly famous, for its his-
tory; but recently it has begun to make history. It
will always be the City of the Confederation, but, in ad-
dition, it is now the City of the Furniture Factory and
the Kilkenny Woollen Mills; and as we cannot live
and stop emigration on history alone, however famous,
it was none too soon that Kilkenny shook itself up, or
rather that some few persons connected with it did so.
About two years and four months ago a woodworkers’
guild was formed. It commenced with two men and a
boy ; it advanced to four men; its total capital was £25,
and it worked in a small way. The guild intended to
do great things, but in five months the difficulties were
too much for it, and, to make a long story short, Cap-
tain the Hon. Otway Cuffe bought out the little enter-
prise, lock, stock and barrel. In twelve months, that
is about a year ago, there were twenty hands working
in what had already become a furniture factory.
There was no machinery at that time, and the period
in the history of the factory was reached when ma-
chinery was necessary if things were to go ahead.
And so machinery—which we will refer to again later
on—was introduced. In July last there were thirty-
four hands, and a wareroom was opened in Dublin at
Nassau Street. On the first of September there were
sixty hands, and about the time of our visit this num-
ber had reached seventy-three, and the need of the
moment is more hands; cabinet makers and polishers
are badly wanted, for it has been decided to fit up a
new cabinet shop and get in more men—if they are to
be had—without delay. The manager at Dublin, Mr.
Earle, is already talking of the time when they will
have 150 or 170 hands; indeed, he sees unlimited pos-
sibilities in this industrial enterprise; he has his heart
in it, and, as he strikes us as nothing. if not a shrewd
business man, with a long training behind him, his
enthusiasm is not of the nebulous kind that vanishes
when facts have to be faced. Some years ago a few
cabinet makers came to our offices and complained to us
of the importation of Scotchmen.  Well, here’s a
chance for Irish cabinet makers now—good steady men
—for Kilkenny is looking for them, and we hope it
will obtain them quickly. (Since this was written we
are informed that nine additional cabinet makers and

two polishers have gone to Kilkenny)..

DROGHEDA AGRICULTURAL AND INDUSTRIAL
SHOW.

FTER a rather lengthened absence I re-visited
A Drogheda on Thursday of last week, the second
day of the Show. I arrived by an early train, and,
having time to spare, I took occasion to renew acquaint-
ance with some old friends. I learned from them that
times were not quite as.good as formerly in the town,
but that local industries were holding their ground
fairly well, particularly the Boyne Spinning and Weav-
ing Co., whick give employment to some ten or twelve
hundred hands, when their mills are working full- time.
The Show is an annual event founded four years ago
by the Drogheda Agricultural Society, and the pro-
gramme includes domestic economy, agricultural and
garden produce, poultry, pigeons, and cage-birds. The
catalogue this year totalled over three thousand entries,
so that in respect of the number of exhibits, the Show
was a great success; and as the total is three times that
of the first year, the event must be taken as having
decidedly caught on to popular favour. The Boys’
Brigade Athletic Grounds, on the North Road,
were availed of for holding the Show, and they are
admirably adapted for the purpose, as they are roomy
and spacious, and quite beside the town. A line of
weoden structures run along tke south and east sides
of the enclosure, and in them were placed the exhibits,
while a commodious stand, erected on the south side,
gave a view of the step-dancing and the horse-jumping
competitions, the former taking place on a platform
immediately in front of the stand, and the latter over a
course in the centre of the grounds.

On entering, the first objects that caught my atten-
tion were Messrs. Doyle and Co.’s (Wexford) exLibition
of agricultural implements. = The most prominent
amongst them, was the LEADER turnip pulper, a very
strong lacerating machine, like its namesake of the
Dublin Press, always so useful and efficient to reduce
to pulp everything in the way of sham and excrescence.
The collection further included ploughs, winnowing
machines, ctc., and made a very creditable display.
Messrs. Pierce and Co. (Wexford) also showed a nice
collection of churns, ploughs, turnip cutters, etc., the
exhibition being by the local agent, Mr. John Collins,
Drogheda. A very interesting, and much the largest,
section was that devoted to poultry, and it comprised
no less than sixty-five classes. It took a long stretch
of canvas roofing to cover all the exhibits, and to a
poultry fancier and expert, the occasion afforded a
splendid opportunity to indulge his taste. Speaking for
myself, T cannot pretend to any knowledge of the
various breeds, and in visiting other shows I have rather
eschewed the poultry section. On this occasion, how-
ever, Laving plenty of time at command, and the Shoy
not being overcrowded at the early hour I attended, I
determined to have a close look at those humble friends
whose species T had so often welcomed on the table. It
was not difficult to find the-arena, if I may so describe
the poultry exhibition, as the place, though bloodless,
resounded on all sides with notes of defiance from the
crowing cocks, inviting each other to mortal combat.

The number of brzeds of poultry exkibited was simply
bewildering, and in some of them there were three dis-
tinct types. ' I considered the Plymouth Rocks showed
up well in their homely check coatings, as a very useful
type altogether devoid of gaudy attractions in the way
of plumage, etc. There appeared to be two types of
Leghorn, and the brown, though small, and not very
taking in appearance, are said to be excellent layers,

WAS 00NNca® RUAD MacnamARa A TOET?

N the present state of public enthusiasm for Irish
l studies there is a strong tendency to over-estimate
the literary merits of our older writers, and sometimes
even to attribute to them qualities which they do not
possess. This over-appreciation by well-known literary
men in the Gaelic movement may be productive of
several bad results. Amongst others, it has the effect
of creating a false standard of taste, and of setting up
for the imitation of young writers literary models of an
inferior character. = One of our older writers, vyhoge
merits as a poet have been grossly exaggerated, is, in
my opinion, Donnca®d Ruad Machamapa. On a 010_“
study of his works, I could not find any indication in
them of what I conceive to be the poetic quality, with
the single exception of his D4&n Cnoic Eijeann O15.
As only a portion of this latter poem is quite original,
namely, the last three stanzas, and as it is but a mere
adaption of an older song, it cannot be considered as
sufficient to entitle a writer to the name of poet. In
examining a writer’s title to poet, we must have a clear
and distinct notion of what we understand by poetry.
Tt is an astonishing fact that in the Ireland of the past,
where so much attention had been given to the com-
position of verse, no thought seems to have been de-
voted to the consideration of the true nature of poetry
in the abstract. All the energies of bards and ollamhs
were occupied with the devising of forms for poetical
expression, with the result that while the form has
been brought to the highest state of perfection, the
matter of Ancient Irish Poetry is often of a very in-
ferior quality, and this constitutes one of its most
serious defects.

What, then, is poetry? Critics have not succeeded
in framing a definition that would only connote the
meaning which the word expresses, but they are abso-
lutely unanimous that poetry is the embodiment of the
work of the imaginative faculty in contradistinction to
that of the intellectual.

Wordsworth, in his famous Preface, says:—‘ Poetry
is the spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings; it
takes its origin from emotion recollected in tran-
quility ; the emotion is contemplated till, by a species
of reaction, the tranquility gradually disappears, and
an emotion kindred to that which was before the sub-
ject of contemplation, is gradually produced.” A
contemporary critic of Donncat Ruao expresses the
same idea in simpler language :—‘ Poetry is poetry be-
cause it is more passionate and sensual than prose. A
discourse that is writ in very good numbers, if it
wants passion, can be but measured prose.’”’

“ Poetry,” says Leigh Hunt, ‘‘ begins where matter
of fact or of science ceases to be merely such, and to
exhibit a further truth; that is to say, the connection
it has with the world of emotion. Inquiring of a
gardener, for instance, what flower it is we see yonder,
he answers, ‘a lily.’ This is a matter of fact. The
botanist pronounces it to be of the order Hezandria
monogymia. This is a matter of science. It is the
“lady’ of the garden, says Spenser and here we begin
to have a poetical sense of its fairness and grace. It
is ‘the plant and flower of light,” says Ben Jonson,
and poetry then shows us the beauty of the flower in
all its mystery and splendour.”

That this is the conception of poetry held by English

\

poets and poets of all great literatures, is abundantly .

evident from their writings. I shall quote some in-
stances from Chaucer, Milton, and Tennyson, illustrat-
ing this essential characteristic of English Poetry, af
three different periods of its growth, at its commence-
ment, in the 17th century, and in our own day.

The following is from Chaucer’s ‘‘ Knight’s Tale * :—
““The busy lark—messenger of day—
Salueth in her song the morrow gray;
And fiery Pheebus riseth up so bright
That all the orient laugheth of the light.””

The rice is first chemically treated and ground up to
the consistency of milk, and the liquor is run off into
tanks, and the rice-fibre, a bye-product, is left behind.
There is quite an enormous area -under tanks in the
factory, and the tanks are divided into two sets. At
each working one set of tanks are filled. ~When the
liquor has been allowed to settle, the gluten—another
bye-product—is decanted off. The deposit is shovelled
out into another tank and washed. Indeed, a great
supply of clear fresh water is one of the chief require-
ments—half a mile from the works there is a great tank
of spring water which is conveyed by piping to the
factory. Two days from the grinding of the rice, and
the various processes and washing, you have solid
cubes of starch. Then the drying commences. The
solid cubes are placed in a hot room at 200 degrees, and
it is calculated that two tons of water is evaporated out
of the 200-degree drying room in one night; afterwards
it is crushed, scraped and undergoes another drying pro-
cess at 90 degrees. In six weeks from the grinding of
the rice the starch is fit for use.

A good deal of the factory space is given over to the
packing and boxmaking departments. The starch is
packed in 1lb., $lb., }1b. packages, and in 1d. and 4d.
packets. The product of the Glanmire starch factory
is done up in various ways. We are all familiar with
the packets of Silverspring starch; there are other
packets inscribed in the Irish language. But Mr.
O’Brien being a business man, knows that, unfor-
tunately, the trumpeting of the fact that a thing is
made in Ireland, handicaps it in certain people’s esti-
mation, and so there are two lines, ‘‘ The Ivory”
packet and ‘ The Mirror ”’ packet that bear no trace
or indication of the place of their origin. Probably
shrewd people who cannot make up a shirt or iron a
cuff with beastly Irish Silverspring starch, are loud in
their praises of the quality of the same product out of*
the same factory when they buy it in packages as
“Ivory” or as ‘‘ Mirror.”

As in most manufacturing businesses now, bye-pro-
ducts form an important item. After careful investi-
gation, the residuum from the rice forms a splendid
article of food for cattle, containing all the nitrogenous
forms of food as well as the albuminoids, it is dried and
pressed into cakes by filter pressers and commands a
very large sale, it is specially recommended as a cake
for poultry, and as rice is not generally used as a food
stuff for animal purposes, it is quite a feature in this
way.

The machinery is worked by water, the wheel repre-
sents about forty-horse power. They have a steam
engine on the vremises, but it was only worked for a
few hours during 25 years, so that it may be taken that
the water at Glanmire is not a fickle friend.

We would be sorry to say that the Convents in Ire-
land that use starch should be compelled to use Irish
starch. They know their business better than we do,
but at the same time we are surprised that they do
not all use it. It is one of those many things in this
abnormal country, which one finds it difficult to under-
stand and impossible to explain, and which often tempt

'[' N my article of last week, referring to the treatment
d of the Irish farm labourer, I remarked that
 habits are not as easily built as Louses.”’ Neither
are tastes. It would be a good thing if our people
could be got to alter their tastes in the matter of some
things they eat and drink. In the matter of intoxi-
cants, for instance, in the drink line, and of American
flour, say, in the eating line. It would also be good if
we could so arrange things that the new tastes the
people from time to time develop should be for articles
we can produce at home. But as practically the only
way to acquire a taste for a thing is first to get the
thing in order that you may taste it, the problem of
controlling the native taste and keeping it native re-
sclves itself into the producing at home of commodities
the taste will like. Of course, you could let the
foreigner invent the new commodity, and then, your
people having acquired a paying taste for it, start the
industry yourself. But that’s just what we are too
disposed to do in Ireland. We are fairly strenuous in
the matter of objecting to take our laws from over the
water, but we are glad to take most things material,
from boots to hats, and from Yorkshire relish or
American bacon to bovril, ready-invented and ready-
made, fit for us or unfit. A disposition, by the way, it
is sufficiently strange the Irishman should be careless
enough to indulge—in regard to commodities intended
for his stomach, at any rate. There was a time when
it was English statesmanship to poison us Irish.

But what I wish to speak about at present is the
possibility of getting up a taste in Ireland for home-
grown fruit and commestibles made therefrom. Jam,
for instance, and cider. TLe latter especially as a sub-
stitute for the. whiskey and porter that are such a tax
on the country. I will confine myself in this article
to the cider question.

TIreland used to be a great apple-producing country.
Over a hundred years ago Arthur Young expressed his
surprise at the luxuriance of the orchards in some of
the Munster counties, Clare, in particular, and Water-
ford. Even as late as forty years ago practically every
farmer in the latter county—in the west of it, at any
rate—had his orchard. Except the gentlemen farmers,
however, I haven’t heard that they used make much
cider of the apples. They sold them to the huxters.
And to show what a demand tkere was for them, the
tradition still lives in the district of the moonlighting
that was resorted to by the more adventurous spirits
to come by a bag of apples, for sale or consumption on
the premises as the case might be, it being_n common
thing to go miles from home, night after night, plun-
dering orchards. So that unless the people’s tastes

have undergone some radical change, it shouldn’t be
difficult to revive the fruit-growing industry in the dis-
tricts where all these apples used find consumers.

As T say, much cider does not appear to have been
made, at least not by the present generation of farmers
or their fathers. Some was made, indeed, some little
of it is still made, but hardly enough to warrant the
calling of the making of it an industry. The question
is, amongst the cottage industries the revival of whick:
is now receiving so much attention, might it be possible
to revive or start this one? As to the desirability of
reviving it if it could be dome there can scarcely be
two opinions.

The matter is one of tke many irons the Department
has in the fire. It is necessarily being handled in a
tentative way, but already a good deal would appear to
have been done in the way of at least getting the farmers,
to think over the business. The first step was the
planting of something like 400 acres of orchard (in plots
of one acre per farmer) spread over various counties.
The produce of these plots—gooseberries, currants and
such small fruit—not being as yet (the trees were
planted a year and a half or so ago) available for sale,
the farmers’ wives and dauglters have been trying their
hands at making jam of them, and as one trade of this
sort horrows another, I am not surprised to hear latterly
that the Department is being asked if it could let cer-
tain districts have the loan of an instructor in cider-
making. As, however, the game could scarcely be
clearly proved to be worth the candle under present
conditions in Ireland, I believe the instructor has not
yet been made available.

Meanwhile in tke Department’s Journal (July) an
article dealing with cider manufacture has been pub-
lished. On that article I propose to hang the remainder
of my remarks on the subject. '

Here is part of the second paragraph of the article.
““ Starting the work (of cider-making),”” it says, ““on a
commercial scale by farmers is not recommended at pre-
sent for the following reasons:—To successfully manu-
facture cider on a commercial scale requires consider-
able capital, a connection amongst the licensed trade,
a permanent staff of men and another business in which
these workers may be profitably employed from
January to May, as cider is only a six or seven montkLs’
trade.”” In other words, if cider-making by the farmers
i3 to be revived, we must expect them, the farmers, to
be satisfied to make only as much of it as will *‘ supply
the house,”” inasmuch as they cannot hope to get rid of
any of it commercially at a profit. And that, further
translated, means that, at a time when it is a growing
custom for farmers to get a pig now and then cured
in the nearest cellar, or to buy cellar bacon in prefer-
ence to killing and saving their own—unless we can get
them, at such a time, to acquire suck a taste for home-
made cider that they will go to the trouble of making
it instead of buying it (or porter or whiskey), we need
not hope to develop cider-making as a rural industry?
Well, if such is the case—and certainly it looks as
though it were—the sooner we start writing and preach-
ing up the virtue and value of home-made cider the
better. I don’t happen to Lave much experience in the
matter myself, but there are some facts in connection
with it plain enough to occur to anyone. I will men-
tion one or two that occur to me.

nevertheless paid for stuff for consumption off the pre-
mises. To keep a family Budget is, statisticians tell

PRIZE DAY.

In the presence of his Lordship the Most Rev. Dr. O'Callaghan,
Bishop of Cork, the Annual Report of St. Angela’s was read, and
at its conclusion his Lordship distributed the various Prizes to tho
successful Pupils.

TWENTIETH ANNUAL REPORT.

In the Royal University of Treland the Collegiate year has been
a brilliant one. At the October Examinations one student took
her degree on the Honour Course in Modern Languages and
Literature. The three students who presented themselves for the
examination in the 2nd Arts were successful. Three out of four
students passed the examination in 1st Arts, one of whom obtained
exceptionally high marks in Mathematics; and 6 Matriculated.
Among those one scored very high marks in Latin, and in German
secured 90 per cent. of the total. In all, 13 students were success-
ful; while at the Examinations now being held, three are pre-
senting themselves for the Honour Degree in Modern Languages.

Notwithstanding the great and unexpected falling off in the
number of distinctions awarded by the Intermediate Board this
year, St. Angela’s School stands first in the South of Ireland, and
the successes were throughout all the Four Grades. The students
who obtained 1st Class Prizes would under last year’s regulations
have been Exhibitioners. The Senior Grade has carried off the
1st Prize for Excellence in the Grade, £5 Prize for German Lan-
_guage and Literature, with 6th place in German, 3rd place in all
TIreland for Drawing (4th Year's Course), 8th place in French, a
very high place in Latin, and 5th place in the Grade, Modern
Literary Course.

The Middle Grade bears off from all Treland the Bronze Medal
for Experimental Science, with the almost -equally coveted dis-
tinction of 1st place, obtaining 248 marks over any other com-
petitor; a 1st Class Prize for Excellence in the Grade, 13th place
in the Modern Literary Course, a £3 Prize for Irish Composition,
with 6th place in the Grade, 4th and 10th places in Drawing (3rd
Year's Course), 2nd place in all Ireland for Geometry, and 9th
place in English Literature and Composition.

In the Junior Grade there is also a 1st Class Prize for Excel-
lence, 3rd place in all Ireland for Irish, losing 2nd place by only
one mark, 14th place in English Literature and Composition, 5th
place in Experimental Science, 1st Year.

The Preparatory ranks high in the Grade, and has 3rd placo
in all Treland for German, 5th placo in History and Geography,
8th place in French. a £3 Prize for Excellence in German.

It wili be noted that succoss in Irish is a prominent feature this
year. As a matter of fact, cent. per cent. of the pupils who went
in for examination in the Middle, Junior and Proparatory Grades
passed, and 99 per cent. of the Senior. Many of these in ecach
Grade were Honour Passes.

In the Choral Class, the musical knowledge shown by the
students was selected for special commendation, and the refine-
ment shown by the Choir, in all the pieces sung, was also noted.

ST. ANGELA’S TRAINIRG COLLEGE.

in connection with the University of Cambridge has justified the

and management of the College have been proved by the results
T( is satisfactory to be able to state that all the students who have
passed so successfully through their year’s training have now
oxcellent appoiniments in the very best secondary schools.  This
proniises well for the future.
